does: This paper trains sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on Qwen3 instruction-tuned language models of various sizes, creating a comprehensive suite of interpretable feature decompositions across multiple layers and activation sites including residual streams, MLP outputs, and attention outputs. The authors systematically evaluate their SAEs using both reconstruction metrics and model-level recovery metrics, characterizing sparsity-fidelity trade-offs across different architectural components. They demonstrate practical utility by showing that specific SAE features can be used to causally steer Qwen3 models toward refusal behavior, establishing that discovered features have genuine functional importance beyond mathematical reconstruction.
connects: This work builds directly on the emerging methodology of sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, extending prior work on feature decomposition in language models. It responds to limitations in previous SAE research by addressing computational bottlenecks and the scarcity of open-source trained models, following foundational work on superposition in neural representations and interpretable feature learning. The paper connects to broader research on understanding and steering language model behavior through mechanistic intervention, building on causal analysis methods and the growing field of techniques for extracting human-interpretable concepts from neural network activations.
enables: This resource enables future work on interpretability and steering of instruction-tuned language models by providing publicly available SAE decompositions across multiple model scales and layers. The demonstrated refusal-steering application points toward practical methods for controlling model behavior through mechanistic feature manipulation, potentially informing safer alignment techniques. The comprehensive evaluation framework and released models facilitate downstream research on understanding how information flows through different architectural components, enable systematic studies of model reasoning processes, and support development of interpretation-based debugging and model editing techniques that could enhance transparency and control of language model systems.
